narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your Year 1 school decile of 6, which is solid and slightly above average, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 below the typical 3.21, your LSAT score of 35 compared to the average 36.70, and your plan to attend full-time like most students—and predicted an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing the model toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of 5.53 and signaled a significant drop in performance that the model sees as a strong warning sign for bar success. Your LSAT score, while respectable, worked slightly in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, as it was close to typical levels among successful students. Your family's upper-middle income also contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, perhaps because the model associates certain income brackets with other patterns in bar outcomes. Compared to typical students who pass, your Year 3 grades stand out as the biggest deviation, pulling you below the profiles of those with consistently strong deciles across years. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average or addressing whatever caused that Year 3 dip—maybe through targeted coursework or tutoring—to strengthen your profile for other schools. Many applicants in similar situations improve by focusing on these academic red flags and reapplying stronger. Your full-time status aligns with most admitted students, so that's a positive foundation to build on. With persistence, you can shift the odds in your favor for future opportunities.
